title: KeyCorp (KEY) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of KeyCorp (KEY). Economic Prospect Score: 55/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A modest 3.0% growth rate reflects near-term challenges in net interest income compression. Growth is heavily dependent on a successful balance sheet restructuring and a gradual recovery in investment banking fees over the medium term.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (Cost of Equity)
discountRate.rationale: A 10.0% cost of equity is used, reflecting the inherent risks in the regional banking sector, including deposit flight risks, commercial real estate exposure, and strict regulatory capital requirements.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.0% terminal growth rate assumes KeyCorp will grow roughly in line with long-term US GDP, characteristic of a mature financial institution operating primarily in domestic markets.

thesisParagraphs: Valuing regional banks like KeyCorp currently requires navigating significant macroeconomic crosscurrents. The recent "higher for longer" interest rate environment has severely penalized the bank, driving up deposit costs faster than loan yields and creating large unrealized losses in its securities portfolio. Consequently, free cash flow generation has been constrained, and net interest margins have suffered. However, the worst of the liquidity fears appear to have subsided.

The strategic investment by Scotiabank is the central pillar of the turnaround thesis. This capital injection significantly derisks KeyCorp's balance sheet, allowing management to accelerate the restructuring of low-yielding investment portfolios without threatening regulatory capital minimums. While earnings growth will be sluggish in the near term as this repositioning occurs, the current valuation reflects excessive pessimism. Trading below its historical tangible book value multiples, KeyCorp presents a value opportunity, provided management successfully executes the restructuring and credit quality in its commercial portfolio remains resilient.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global.

faq.question: Why is the discount rate relatively high at 10%?
faq.answer: Regional banks face elevated systemic risks, regulatory burdens, and potential credit cycle deterioration, warranting a higher cost of equity to compensate for these uncertainties.

faq.question: How does the Scotiabank investment affect valuation?
faq.answer: It significantly lowers liquidity and capital risk, providing a floor to the valuation, even though the issuance of new shares dilutes existing equity holders.

faq.question: What is the biggest risk to this valuation?
faq.answer: A sharp recession causing widespread defaults in commercial real estate or middle-market business loans, which would decimate book value and earnings.

prospectVerdictDetail: KeyCorp is a prominent regional bank navigating a challenging interest rate environment and heightened regulatory scrutiny. While its commercial banking franchise and wealth management advisory services provide a solid foundation, funding costs remain elevated, compressing net interest margins. The strategic capital injection from Scotiabank shores up its balance sheet and liquidity position, providing a pathway to stabilize earnings and restructure its securities portfolio, supporting a moderate outlook.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: Competitive momentum is weak, hindered by margin compression and sluggish loan demand. The bank is primarily focused on balance sheet optimization rather than aggressive market share expansion.

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs. Peers
factors.rationale: KeyCorp's revenue growth has significantly lagged larger national banks. High deposit beta and the cost of maintaining liquidity have severely pressured Net Interest Income (NII), though non-interest income from investment banking shows sporadic strength.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: Market share is relatively stagnant. Regional banks broadly face intense competition for deposits from money market funds and "too big to fail" institutions, limiting organic growth opportunities.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: Pricing power is constrained. KeyCorp has had to aggressively raise deposit rates to retain clients, compressing the spread it can earn on its loan portfolio in a 'higher for longer' rate environment.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: Product offerings are standard for a super-regional bank. While digital transformation efforts are ongoing, they are defensive measures to retain parity rather than disruptive innovations driving new customer acquisition.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: Moat durability relies on established middle-market commercial relationships and a sticky retail deposit base in core regional markets, though the moat is inherently narrow.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: Switching costs for retail banking are moderate due to automated payments and direct deposits. For commercial clients deeply integrated with KeyCorp's treasury management and advisory services, switching costs are higher and provide relationship stickiness.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: Traditional regional banking possesses minimal network effects. A larger branch footprint offers convenience but does not exponentially increase the platform's value to individual users in the digital age.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: As a large regional bank, KeyCorp faces stringent regulatory capital and liquidity requirements (Basel III endgame). The banking charter itself serves as a barrier to entry, but increasing regulatory burdens weigh heavily on compliance costs.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: Banking is inherently capital intensive. KeyCorp's recent move to raise equity capital highlights the constant need to maintain robust capital ratios to absorb potential credit losses and satisfy regulatory demands.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: Sentiment is cautiously optimistic following the Scotiabank investment, which removed near-term capital concerns, but fundamental earnings recovery remains a "show me" story.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: Earnings estimates have seen downward revisions due to persistent NII headwinds. Analysts are waiting for clear signs that net interest margin has troughed before forecasting meaningful earnings expansion.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative is dominated by the strategic investment from Scotiabank. This is viewed positively as a derisking event, shifting the focus from liquidity fears toward the potential for future capital deployment and restructuring.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Management's decision to dilute shareholders to secure capital from Scotiabank was a necessary defensive move to optimize the balance sheet. Future capital allocation will be strictly focused on regulatory compliance and restructuring low-yielding assets.

prospectKeyRisks: A severe macroeconomic recession leading to elevated credit defaults, particularly within the commercial real estate (CRE) portfolio.
Persistent 'higher for longer' interest rates continuing to drive up deposit costs, further compressing net interest margins.
Increased regulatory capital requirements forcing the bank to hold more capital, diluting Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE).
prospectKeyCatalysts: Successful restructuring of the underwater securities portfolio, utilizing the Scotiabank capital injection to improve future net interest income.
A normalized yield curve and falling short-term interest rates, which would rapidly relieve funding cost pressures and expand margins.
A rebound in capital markets activity boosting non-interest income from investment banking and advisory fees.
prospectMethodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored KEY at 54/100 and Opus at 56/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
